column2: Celestial divination continues with limited astronomical knowledge.
Enuma Anu Enlil compiled, containing 7,000 celestial omens on 70 cuneiform tablets.
Astronomy remains rudimentary; interpretation occurs as phenomena happen.
Mathematical methods advance, allowing predictions of future planetary positions with more accuracy.
Oldest known texts of Babylonian divination, showing the same interpretational format as celestial omen analysis.
Celestial bodies (planets and stars) associated with gods; evil omens seen as signs of divine dissatisfaction.
Used to mitigate negative omens, such as during eclipses, by performing symbolic rituals or actions.
Astrology mixed theistic and mechanical worldviews, emphasizing celestial events without the notion of revenge.

column3: Marduk was the patron god of Babylon, associated with the planet Jupiter and considered the most powerful god.
The goddess of love and war, Venus was tied to Ishtar in Babylonian astrology.
Saturn was linked to the god of agriculture and healing, Ninurta.
Mercury was associated with Nabu, the god of wisdom and writing.
Mars was associated with Nergal, the god of war, destruction, and the underworld.

column1: 5. Limits of Early Knowledge in Babylonian Astrology

column2: Early astrology focused on public welfare and the well-being of the king, not on individual horoscopes.
Early Babylonian astronomy was rudimentary; the system of dividing the sky into 12 constellations with 30° each wasn't perfected until later.
Priests used astrology for practical divination rather than in-depth scientific study, based on traditional interpretations of celestial events.

The Ashta Bhairavas (Sanskrit: अष्टभैरवः, romanized: Aṣṭabhairavah, lit. 'eight Bhairavas') are the eight manifestations of the Hindu god Bhairava,[1] a ferocious form of Shiva. They are regarded to guard and control the eight cardinal directions. Each of the Ashta Bhairavas is regarded to preside over eight subordinate Bhairavas, totalling 64 Bhairavas.[2] The Ashta Bhairavas are described to be subordinate to Kālabhairava, who is considered the supreme ruler of time in the universe and the chief form of Bhairava.
